## [Ep. 372: Kant’s Ethics Lectures (Part Two for Supporters)](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2025/07/27/ep372-2-kant-ethics-lectures-citizen/)

July 27, 2025 by [Mark Linsenmayer](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/author/marklinsenmayer/)

[](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2025/07/27/ep372-2-kant-ethics-lectures-citizen/)

Continuing from part one on the 1785 course lecture notes by Georg Ludwig Collins, as collected in the Cambridge edition.
We cover "Of Duties to Oneself," which are actually the primary ethical duties. This is not a matter of pursuing your desires, but recognizing your inherent dignity, and respecting that. When you do this, you'll have integrity, and will be in a good [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2025/07/27/ep372-2-kant-ethics-lectures-citizen/)

## [Ep. 372: Kant’s Ethics Lectures (Part One for Supporters)](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2025/07/26/ep372-1-kant-ethics-lectures-citizen/)

We originally discussed Kant's ethics via his Groundwork (also 1785) back in our ep. 10 and wanted to revisit the ideas given our recent work on [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2025/07/26/ep372-1-kant-ethics-lectures-citizen/)

## [The Inescapable Imperative](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2025/02/01/the-inescapable-imperative/)

February 1, 2025 by [Jay Jeffers](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/author/jay-jeffers/) [Leave a Comment](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2025/02/01/the-inescapable-imperative/)

[](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2025/02/01/the-inescapable-imperative/)

Moral reasons are strong. That's what you'd expect, at least. They're weighty but not dull, alive but not hedonistic. It's strange then that the term used time and again to describe the ultimate importance of moral reasoning is "objective," a word so bloodless and clinical. Still, there's something there that attempts to articulate the bottom-line concern. It's understandable, [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2025/02/01/the-inescapable-imperative/)

## [Ep. 321: August Schlegel on Beauty (Part One for Supporters)](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2023/07/08/ep321-1-august-schlegel-citizen/)

July 8, 2023 by [Mark Linsenmayer](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/author/marklinsenmayer/)

[](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2023/07/08/ep321-1-august-schlegel-citizen/)

Covering the elder Schlegel brother's Theory of Art (ca. 1800), as excerpted in Theory as Practice: A Critical Anthology of Early German Romantic Writings.
August Wilhelm Schlegel was five years older than Friedrich, and was also a well known art critic of his time. This text is a Romantic response to Kant's Third Critique, and we looked back at Kant's comments on the [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2023/07/08/ep321-1-august-schlegel-citizen/)

## [Ep. 318: Friedrich Schiller on the Civilizing Potential of Art (Part One for Supporters)](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2023/05/28/ep318-1-schiller-art-education-citizen/)

May 28, 2023 by [Mark Linsenmayer](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/author/marklinsenmayer/)

[](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2023/05/28/ep318-1-schiller-art-education-citizen/)

Can art make us better people? Musician Markus Reuter joins Mark, Wes, and Seth to discussion the first half of On the Aesthetic Education of Man (1795). Schiller was a famous poet of early German Romanticism, and this book is partly political philosophy and partly philosophy of art. The work takes the form of a series of letters. We read 1-15 for this discussion and will cover [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2023/05/28/ep318-1-schiller-art-education-citizen/)

## [Ep. 295: Kant on Preventing War (Part Three for Supporters)](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/06/11/ep295-3-kant-perpetual-peace-citizen/)

June 11, 2022 by [Mark Linsenmayer](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/author/marklinsenmayer/)

[](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/06/11/ep295-3-kant-perpetual-peace-citizen/)

Concluding on Kant's "Perpetual Peace," plus Jurgen Habermas' "Kant's Idea of Perpetual Peace, with the Benefit of Two Hundred Years' Hindsight." Start with part one.
We talk about the two appendices to Kant's essay: first about "realpolitik," the idea that because other states will act immorally, then the wise politician must also act immorally. As you might predict, Kant [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/06/11/ep295-3-kant-perpetual-peace-citizen/)

## [Ep. 295: Kant on Preventing War (Part Two for Supporters)](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/06/05/ep295-2-kant-perpetual-peace-citizen/)

June 5, 2022 by [Mark Linsenmayer](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/author/marklinsenmayer/)

[](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/06/05/ep295-2-kant-perpetual-peace-citizen/)

Continuing from part one on Immanuel Kant's essay "Perpetual Peace," we go further into how Kant's politics relate to his ethics and consider his actual policy proposals: each state must be a republic, i.e. somehow representative with separation of powers, and countries should join in a confederation. Kant also spells out the new idea of "cosmopolitan right," which only entails [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/06/05/ep295-2-kant-perpetual-peace-citizen/)

## [Ep. 295: Kant on Preventing War (Part One for Supporters)](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/06/05/ep295-1-kant-perpetual-peace-citizen/)

June 5, 2022 by [Mark Linsenmayer](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/author/marklinsenmayer/)

[](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/06/05/ep295-1-kant-perpetual-peace-citizen/)

On Immanuel Kant's essay "Perpetual Peace: A Philosophical Sketch" (1795). Do nations have the "right" to go to war? What principles ground just international relations, and are there structures and agreements that we can embrace to prevent prevent future wars? Naturally, we consider the current conflict in Ukraine as well as other recent wars.
Kant's essay reads like a [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/06/05/ep295-1-kant-perpetual-peace-citizen/)

## [Ep. 287: Roger Scruton on Beauty (Part Two for Supporters)](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/02/13/ep287-2-scruton-beauty-citizen/)

February 13, 2022 by [Mark Linsenmayer](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/author/marklinsenmayer/)

[](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/02/13/ep287-2-scruton-beauty-citizen/)

Continuing from part one on Beauty (2009), ch. 1-4. We critically examine Scruton's claim that apprehending beauty is cognitive and never merely sensory, which would rule out, e.g. there being beautiful smells or tastes.
We also go into points from Scruton's chapters on natural beauty, human beauty, and everyday beauty. Appreciation of natural beauty seems to be something [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2022/02/13/ep287-2-scruton-beauty-citizen/)

Continuing from part one our close reading of The Phenomenology of Spirit (1807), ch. 3, "Force and the Understanding."
We start off with the dynamic between the expressed and merely stored up aspects of force and how this relates to the forcing and the forced entities in the interaction. Which of these is the "solicitor" and which is being solicited? Either one can be seen [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2021/08/29/ep277-2-hegel-physics-citizen/)

## [Ep. 200: Kant/Mendelssohn/Foucault on Enlightenment (Part Two)](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2018/10/15/ep200-2-kant-mendelssohn-foucault/)

October 15, 2018 by [Mark Linsenmayer](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/author/marklinsenmayer/) [5 Comments](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2018/10/15/ep200-2-kant-mendelssohn-foucault/)

[](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2018/10/15/ep200-2-kant-mendelssohn-foucault/)

Continuing on "What Is Enlightenment" by Immanuel Kant (1784), "On Enlightening the Mind" by Moses Mendelssohn (1784), and "What Is Enlightenment" by Michael Foucault (1984).
We finish up Kant (the courage to know!) and lay out the Mendelssohn (cultivation vs. enlightenment) and Foucault (ironically heroize the present!). Will this conversation enlighten you? Who [Continue Reading …](https://partiallyexaminedlife.com/2018/10/15/ep200-2-kant-mendelssohn-foucault/)

At the end of the historical period known as the Enlightenment, a Berlin newspaper asked what exactly that is, and Kant and Mendelssohn both responded (neither having read the other's answer). #### About The Partially Examined Life

The Partially Examined Life is a philosophy podcast by some guys who were at one point set on doing philosophy for a living but then thought better of it. Each episode, we pick a text and chat about it with some balance between insight and flippancy. You don’t have to know any philosophy, or even to have read the text we’re talking about to (mostly) follow and (hopefully) enjoy the discussion

#### Become a PEL Citizen!

PEL Citizens have access to all podcast episodes, free access to podcast transcripts, guided readings, episode guides, PEL music, and other citizen-exclusive material. Click [here](http://partiallyexaminedlife.com/membership-options/) to join.
